structured_choices.data_source: Patient vital sign records from hospital EHR system, two-year period (24 months), ~10,000 patient admissions. Raw signals: heart rate, blood pressure (systolic/diastolic), oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, temperature — all timestamped at regular intervals (assumed hourly or per clinical documentation). No exclusion criteria applied yet; baseline cohort is all admissions with complete vital sign sequences and a labeled sepsis outcome.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: sepsis onset (1) vs. no sepsis (0). Sepsis is defined using the reference standard from EHR sepsis alerts and/or clinical consensus criteria (e.g., qSOFA, SIRS + infection). The label timestamp is the time of confirmed sepsis diagnosis or ICU transfer/clinical escalation. For negative cases, the label is assigned at the end of the admission or at a fixed reference point (e.g., 48 hours post-admission if no sepsis was documented).
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by sepsis outcome. The two-year dataset is divided into three chronological windows: months 1–12 (training set, ~60% of admissions), months 13–15 (validation set, ~20%), and months 16–24 (test set, ~20%). This temporal split is mandatory because the task is prospective prediction: the model must be trained on historical data and evaluated on future (held-out) temporal windows to avoid data leakage and to simulate deployment conditions. Within each window, admissions are stratified by sepsis/no-sepsis to ensure the same sepsis incidence rate across splits. This addresses class imbalance at the split level and ensures fair representation in validation/test.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, feature normalization) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–12). Fitted parameters (mean, std, quantiles, encoding mappings) are then applied without refitting to the validation and test sets. Missing vital sign values within each patient's time series are imputed using forward-fill (last observation carried forward) within a 2-hour window, followed by linear interpolation if gaps persist; this is fit on the training set to establish imputation rules and applied identically to val/test. No information from validation or test data influences the training preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: For each patient admission, construct a multivariate time series of vital signs over the 24 hours preceding the label time (or preceding the end of admission for negative cases). Raw features: heart rate, systolic BP, diastolic BP, mean arterial pressure (derived), oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, temperature. Each feature is standardized (z-score) using training set parameters. Additionally, engineer temporal features: rate of change (delta) of each vital over 1-hour and 4-hour windows, rolling mean and standard deviation (2-hour window). Do not include any features derived from sepsis diagnosis codes, culture results, or antibiotics — these would be future information. Static admission features (age, sex, prior comorbidities) are included but not used as the sole basis for comparison; the TCN operates on the temporal signal.
structured_choices.model: Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) with the following architecture: 2–3 dilated convolutional layers (dilation rates 1, 2, 4 to capture multi-scale temporal patterns), kernel size 3, 64–128 filters per layer, ReLU activations, dropout (0.2–0.3) for regularization, and a final dense layer with sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameter tuning (layer depth, filter count, dropout, learning rate, batch size) is performed on the validation set using grid search or random search with 50–100 trials. Early stopping is used with the validation set to prevent overfitting.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on static aggregate features derived from the vital sign sequence: mean, min, max, and standard deviation of each vital over the 24-hour window, plus static admission features (age, sex, comorbidity count). The logistic regression is given equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength (L1/L2, alpha) is tuned via cross-validation within the training set using the same validation set metric. Both the TCN and baseline are trained with the same class weights to account for sepsis class imbalance. The baseline represents the current standard practice (simple statistical summaries + logistic regression) and is evaluated on the same test set.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Sensitivity (recall, true positive rate) at a fixed threshold that ensures specificity ≥ 90%. This metric is chosen because the hospital's stated priority is reducing sepsis-related morbidity and mortality: missing a sepsis case (false negative) is clinically more costly than a false alarm. Sensitivity is the appropriate metric for this stakeholder objective. The threshold is selected on the validation set to maximize sensitivity subject to the specificity floor; this threshold is then applied to the test set without adjustment.
secondary_metrics: Specificity (true negative rate) to ensure the model does not over-alert clinicians, leading to alert fatigue.
Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) to assess overall discrimination across all thresholds.
Precision to quantify the positive predictive value and clinical utility of raised alerts.
F1 score (macro) as a secondary balance metric.
Calibration plot (predicted probability vs. actual incidence) to ensure the model's confidence estimates are reliable for clinical decision-support.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and model selection are performed using the validation set (months 13–15) only. The best-performing hyperparameter configuration is selected based on the primary metric (sensitivity at ≥90% specificity) on the validation set. Once selected, the model is evaluated on the held-out test set (months 16–24) only once, at the end, to obtain the final performance report. The test set is never used for tuning or early stopping.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 16–24) is held completely separate and used only once at the end of the experiment to report final performance. No test set data is used for hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, or decision-threshold calibration. All development (feature engineering refinement, model architecture choice, threshold selection) is performed on training + validation data only.
structured_choices.confound_controls: To account for external confounds (medication changes, hospital protocol shifts during the two-year period), stratify the analysis by calendar quarter or clinical protocol version if that metadata is available. Report performance separately for each quarter to detect any temporal drift or protocol-induced performance variation. Additionally, perform a sensitivity analysis: refit the models using only months 13–24 (excluding months 1–12) to check whether results are robust to potential long-term data drift. Flag any significant performance differences as evidence of confounding. Document any known protocol changes or medication guideline updates that occurred during the data collection period and note whether they correlate with performance shifts. For class imbalance, use stratified sampling in the temporal split (already specified above) and apply class weights during model training in both TCN and baseline.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: TCN vs. Logistic Regression for 24-Hour Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use the full two-year EHR dataset (approximately 10,000 admissions) with timestamped vital sign records: heart rate, blood pressure (systolic and diastolic), oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, and temperature. The sepsis outcome is defined using the hospital's clinical consensus criteria (e.g., qSOFA score, SIRS criteria with culture evidence, or sepsis alert flags in the EHR). The label timestamp marks confirmed sepsis onset; for non-sepsis cases, the reference time is the end of admission or a fixed 48-hour post-admission window.

### 2. Temporal Train–Validation–Test Split

To prevent future information leakage and to simulate the prospective deployment scenario, we divide the dataset chronologically: months 1–12 form the training set (~60%, ~6,000 admissions), months 13–15 form the validation set (~20%, ~2,000 admissions), and months 16–24 form the held-out test set (~20%, ~2,000 admissions). Within each temporal window, we stratify by sepsis outcome to ensure consistent class prevalence across splits. This temporal split is essential because the hypothesis concerns prospective prediction 24 hours ahead; a random split would allow the model to "see" future vital sign patterns during training, invalidating the test.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For each patient, we construct a multivariate time series consisting of vital signs recorded over the 24-hour window preceding the outcome label. We apply missing-value imputation (forward-fill within 2 hours, then linear interpolation) using rules derived from the training set only. We standardize all vital signs (z-score normalization) using training set parameters (mean, std). We engineer temporal derivatives: rate of change over 1-hour and 4-hour windows, plus rolling mean and standard deviation (2-hour windows). We exclude any features derived from sepsis diagnostic codes, antibiotic orders, or culture results, as these represent information that would not be available prospectively. All preprocessing transformers are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Baseline Model

The logistic regression baseline operates on static aggregate features: mean, min, max, and standard deviation of each vital sign over the 24-hour window, plus static admission covariates (age, sex, comorbidity count). This baseline represents current clinical practice—statistical summaries combined with simple linear classification. We tune the logistic regression's regularization strength (L1/L2, alpha parameter) using the same validation set and metric as the TCN, ensuring equivalent tuning effort and fair comparison.

### 5. Model Training and Selection

We train a temporal convolutional network (TCN) with 2–3 dilated convolutional layers (dilation rates 1, 2, 4), kernel size 3, 64–128 filters, ReLU activations, and dropout (0.2–0.3). Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using grid search or random search (50–100 trials) to optimize the primary metric. Early stopping is used during training to prevent overfitting. Both TCN and logistic regression are trained with class weights inversely proportional to class frequency to account for sepsis rarity.

### 6. Evaluation and Metrics

The primary metric is sensitivity (true positive rate) at a specificity floor of ≥90%, selected on the validation set and fixed before evaluating the test set. This metric directly aligns with the hospital's objective: reducing sepsis-related mortality by minimizing false negatives (missed cases). Secondary metrics include specificity, AUROC, precision, F1 score, and calibration plots to ensure clinical reliability. Both models are evaluated on the identical test set (months 16–24) without any test set participation in tuning or threshold selection.

### 7. Confound and Sensitivity Analysis

We stratify performance reporting by calendar quarter to detect any impact from medication guideline changes or hospital protocol shifts during the two-year period. We document all known protocol changes and correlate them with performance trends. Additionally, we refit both models using only months 13–24 (omitting months 1–12) as a sensitivity check for temporal drift. Significant performance divergence flags potential confounding and is reported transparently.

### 8. Test Set Integrity

The test set is held completely separate and evaluated only once, at the conclusion of all development work. No test data informs hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, or threshold calibration. This single-evaluation policy ensures that test set results are a genuine estimate of generalization performance.
